---
title: "Project 2.2 — Classification · INSTRUCTOR SOLUTION"
subtitle: "From Inference to Discovery · Gate A · 2 of 2"
author: "Reference solution (instructor-only)"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-fold: false
    embed-resources: true
execute:
  warning: false
jupyter: python3
---

::: {.callout-important}
## Instructor-only reference solution
Do **not** distribute. Implementations in `solutions/_reference.py`; see
`solutions/VALIDATION.md`.
:::

```{python}
import sys, os
sys.path.insert(0, os.path.abspath(os.path.join(os.getcwd(), "..")))
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from info521 import data, plotting
import _reference as ref

plotting.use_course_style()
ds = data.load_clinical()
X_raw, feat = ds["X"], ds["features"]
labels, rule = data.hypertension(ds)
```

## 0 · Reuse the split; map labels to ±1

```{python}
Xtr_r, Xte_r, ytr, yte = ref.train_test_split(X_raw, labels, test_frac=0.25, seed=521)
mu, sd = ref.standardize_fit(Xtr_r)
Xtr = ref.standardize_apply(Xtr_r, mu, sd)
Xte = ref.standardize_apply(Xte_r, mu, sd)
ttr = np.where(ytr == 1, 1, -1); tte = np.where(yte == 1, 1, -1)
base = max(yte.mean(), 1 - yte.mean())
print(f"majority-class baseline accuracy = {base:.3f}")
```

## 1 · Soft-margin SVM (Pegasos, from scratch)

```{python}
w, b = ref.svm_fit_pegasos(Xtr, ttr, lam=1e-4, n_epochs=50, seed=521)  # C ≈ 1/(N·lam)
print(f"margin width 2/‖w‖ = {ref.margin_width(w):.2f}")
print(f"support vectors (t·f ≤ 1): {ref.n_support_vectors(Xtr, ttr, w, b)} / {len(ttr)}")
print("weights:", dict(zip(feat, np.round(w, 3))), f"bias={b:.3f}")
```

Most points are support vectors — the classes overlap heavily in this six-feature
space, so the soft margin is wide and many points sit inside it.

## 2 · Precision, recall, F1 (from scratch)

```{python}
svm_pred01 = (ref.svm_predict(Xte, w, b) == 1).astype(int)
P, R, F1 = ref.precision_recall_f1(yte, svm_pred01)
print(f"SVM  test: acc={ref.accuracy(tte, ref.svm_predict(Xte, w, b)):.3f}  "
      f"P={P:.3f}  R={R:.3f}  F1={F1:.3f}")
```

Accuracy alone is misleading (classes ≈ 61/39), so we report precision/recall/F1: the
SVM trades recall to keep precision, landing F1 ≈ 0.40.

## 3 · The dual and the kernel trick

Lagrange multipliers $\alpha_n$ on the margin constraints give the dual
$$
\max_{\boldsymbol\alpha}\ \sum_n\alpha_n
-\tfrac12\sum_{n,m}\alpha_n\alpha_m t_n t_m\,\mathbf x_n^\top\mathbf x_m,
\quad 0\le\alpha_n\le C,\ \sum_n\alpha_n t_n=0 .
$$
Only points on/inside the margin get $\alpha_n>0$ (the **support vectors**); the rest
do not enter the solution. The data appear **only** through inner products
$\mathbf x_n^\top\mathbf x_m$, so replacing them with a kernel
$k(\mathbf x_n,\mathbf x_m)$ fits a linear boundary in an implicit high-dimensional
space — a nonlinear boundary here — without ever forming that space. **Would it help?**
2.1 showed the classes overlap and a linear logistic model only reaches AUC ≈ 0.65;
an RBF kernel might bend the boundary slightly, but the limiting factor is *weak
features*, not boundary shape — so we expect modest gains at best. (A kernelized
implementation is the optional stretch; not run here.)

## 4 · Probabilistic vs margin, same split

```{python}
# refit the 2.1 probabilistic model on the same split
Xtr_i = ref.add_intercept(Xtr); Xte_i = ref.add_intercept(Xte)
w_map, _ = ref.fit_map_newton(Xtr_i, ytr.astype(float), tau2=100.0)
proba = ref.predict_proba(Xte_i, w_map)
log_pred01 = (proba >= 0.5).astype(int)
Pl, Rl, Fl = ref.precision_recall_f1(yte, log_pred01)

print(f"Logistic (thr 0.5): acc={ref.accuracy(yte, log_pred01):.3f}  "
      f"P={Pl:.3f}  R={Rl:.3f}  F1={Fl:.3f}")
print(f"SVM               : acc={ref.accuracy(yte, svm_pred01):.3f}  "
      f"P={P:.3f}  R={R:.3f}  F1={F1:.3f}")
print(f"prediction agreement = {(svm_pred01 == log_pred01).mean():.3f}  "
      f"(so they are NOT the same boundary)")
```

**When does each win? (≈220 words).** On this cohort the two are close in aggregate —
logistic F1 ≈ 0.42, SVM F1 ≈ 0.40 — yet they **disagree on ~20% of test patients**,
so they are genuinely different classifiers, not a relabelled boundary. The
difference is in what each *expresses*. The Bayesian logistic model returns a
calibrated **probability** with a posterior **uncertainty** (from 2.1): it can say "this
patient is 0.55 likely hypertensive, and I am not very sure," and its threshold can be
moved *after* fitting to trade precision against recall for a given clinical cost. The
SVM returns a **geometric** verdict — which side of the max-margin hyperplane — with no
native probability; its only knob, $C$, is set *before* fitting and reshapes the model
itself. For a screening tool where you want to rank patients by risk and tune the
alert threshold to clinic capacity, the probabilistic model wins. For a one-shot
decision with a fixed operating point and no need for calibrated risk, the SVM's
crisp, max-margin boundary is simpler and just as accurate. Here, neither is strong:
six non-BP features carry only weak signal about a BP-defined label (by design — BP is
excluded to avoid leakage), so the honest conclusion is that *the framing choice
matters more than the few points of F1 between them*.

---

## Play artifact — the threshold sweep & the $C$ knob

```{python}
fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(11, 4))

# (1) probabilistic model: precision-recall tradeoff across thresholds
thrs = np.linspace(0.05, 0.95, 40)
precs, recs = [], []
for thr in thrs:
    p, r, _ = ref.precision_recall_f1(yte, (proba >= thr).astype(int))
    precs.append(p); recs.append(r)
axL.plot(recs, precs, marker=".", color=plotting.OKABE_ITO["blue"])
p5, r5, _ = ref.precision_recall_f1(yte, (proba >= 0.5).astype(int))
axL.scatter([r5], [p5], color="black", zorder=5, label="thr = 0.5")
axL.set_xlabel("recall"); axL.set_ylabel("precision")
axL.set_title("logistic precision–recall tradeoff"); axL.legend()

# (2) SVM: vary C (= 1/(N·lam)); watch margin, #SVs, test error
lams = np.logspace(-1, -5, 9)
margins, nsv, errs = [], [], []
for lam in lams:
    wl, bl = ref.svm_fit_pegasos(Xtr, ttr, lam=lam, n_epochs=40, seed=521)
    margins.append(ref.margin_width(wl))
    nsv.append(ref.n_support_vectors(Xtr, ttr, wl, bl))
    errs.append(1 - ref.accuracy(tte, ref.svm_predict(Xte, wl, bl)))
Cs = 1.0 / (len(ttr) * lams)
axR.semilogx(Cs, margins, marker="o", label="margin width")
axR.semilogx(Cs, np.array(errs)*20, marker="s", label="test error ×20")
axR.set_xlabel("C = 1/(N·λ)"); axR.set_title("SVM: C narrows the margin"); axR.legend()
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()

print("threshold 0.5 operating point: P=%.3f R=%.3f" % (p5, r5))
print("low thr (0.3) recall:", round(ref.precision_recall_f1(yte,(proba>=0.3).astype(int))[1], 3))
```

**Reading (≈170 words).** The **threshold sweep** moves the operating point of a
*single fixed* probabilistic model: pushing the threshold down from 0.5 toward 0.3
trades precision for recall — recall climbs from ~0.36 to ~0.83 — tracing the
precision–recall curve. Nothing about the model changes; only the decision boundary on
its probability output moves. The **$C$ knob**, by contrast, changes the *model*:
large $C$ (small $\lambda$) makes margin violations expensive, so the optimizer
narrows the margin ($2/\lVert\mathbf w\rVert$ shrinks) to fit the training data more
tightly; small $C$ widens the margin and tolerates violations. These are two different
levers students routinely conflate: one re-reads a fixed model at a new cutoff, the
other refits a new model with a different bias–variance posture. Here both leave test
accuracy near the 0.61 baseline, underscoring that the six non-BP features — not the
classifier or its tuning — are the binding constraint.

::: {.callout-note}
## Gate A
Gate A complete when 2.1 + 2.2 Satisfactory **and Checkpoint 4** passed.
:::

## Looking ahead

Gate B drops the label entirely: k-means on the same six features, then PCA.
